txpartsupport: [i]NOTE: (β) This language is only partially supported. Therefore, some sections are in English. Support is welcome (Github). - On the last page, you will find an [ref=autotr][u]‘Auto-Translate’[/u][/ref] button[/i]
txnosupport: [i]NOTE: Your language is not (yet) supported, so English is shown. An [ref=autotr][u]‘Auto-Translate’[/u][/ref] button is available on the last page[/i]
txautotranslate: [i]NOTE: (α) This language is not (yet) supported. This is a machine translation. (Auto-translated languages may be removed in future versions.)[/i]
txanleitung: [DF]{LANGSUPPORT}1. Enable the checkbox {txgpsauto} to automatically update your location during use. ({txgpsbutton} determines your current position once and stores it.) Nothing more is required for now. – For higher accuracy see {quotes|0-}4. {txshowagni-}{quotes|1-}.[~LF][~LF]Alternatively, you may enter a place name or an address into the text field (if supported by your device) or coordinates (e.g. 50.94, 6.96). A 3rd and 4th entry are optional: approximate altitude &bl;m], approximate temperature at twilight &bl;°C]. Then enable the checkbox {txwelcomescr}. – When basic data are changed, {txsunclock-} performs a daily calculation which may take between 1[NDASH]30 seconds depending on the device.[~LF][~LF]2. {txshowclock} displays a digital clock at the upper right of the dial. {txuhrsinn} defines whether the clock runs clockwise (south) or counter-clockwise: when changing the direction of rotation, the part of the solar circle below the horizon is positioned in front of you (details see next page).
txmore: [DF]3. {txnachtmodus} {txlangarr|auto-} switches at sunset, at −6° until the end of twilight, +6° before it. {txfarben} selects different dials.[~LF][~LF]4. {txfullscr} prevents the screen from turning off and switches to full screen. {txweathapi} overlays a forecast on the clock (see below, network required). {txagnihotra} shows markers for the solar hand as well as the time of the next solar zero crossing & countdown. Six minutes before Agnihotra the display is enlarged and seconds are shown (⁺ = GPS current). If your settings are sometimes not applied, tap the [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]⟳[/font] button at the bottom right on the dial.[~LF][~LF]You can zoom into the dial with two fingers (or with a triple tap). If you want to move the zoomed image, do it slowly. Fast movements swipe to the next page. - Use the [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]✸[/font] button to activate {txtimecontrol} (see next page).
txlegende: [DF][b]–– Legend:[/b][~LF][~LF]Weather: from bottom to top, current temperature, daily precipitation data (χ = snow) and hours of sunshine; for symbols, see the next page[DF][DF][DF]
txlocdata: [DF]Calculations for your location correspond with the astronomical almanac to approx. 0.0005 arcseconds³:[DF]
txobjdata: – Sun¹ –[~LF]Time: {sun_clocktm}[~LF]Temporal hour: {sun_temptm}[~LF]Daylight: {sun_daylight}{sun_lighttime}{sun_agnicountdown}{sun_agnihotra}[~LF]Solstice: {sun_soli}[~LF]Equinox: {sun_equi}[~LF][~LF]– Moon² –[~LF]Lunar night: {moon_gordon}[~LF]Illumination: {moon_illum}[~LF]Size: {moon_sizep} (85-100)[~LF]New Moon: {moon_new}[~LF]Full Moon: {moon_full}{weather_data}[~LF][~LF]– Brightness –[~LF]Venus: {venus_magp} (83-100)[~LF]Mars: {mars_magp} (52-100)[~LF]Jupiter: {jupiter_magp} (87-100)[~LF]Saturn: {saturn_magp} (77-100)[~LF]Mercury: {merkur_magp} (0-100)[~LF][~LF]¹ Time: GPS = sync | Temporal hour: local unequal hour (0.0[NDASH]11.9), {s_td} day / {s_tn} night. {s_tv} night watch (0{s_tv-}00[NDASH]3{s_tv-}59) | {lighttime-}: see below | Daylight & Agnihotra: sunrise[NDASH]sunset today {sb0}sunrise tomorrow{sb1}, ⁺ = current GPS | ² Full/New Moon: moment (in days/days ago) | Lunar night: see below | ³ Moon ≈ 2000 arcsec | ⁴ Weather: Daily progression ([font=DejaVuSans.ttf]☀[/font] sun, [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]◴[/font] progress, ₁₂₃ = current 3h block, [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]☂[/font] rain clickable = radar, [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]✈[/font] gusts). Now = next 3 hours, [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]◵[/font] = older data. Display limited to 48 hours for reliability | Below daily values: min...max...{sb0}night min{sb1} ({s_sn-} = snow)[DF][DF]
txlegende0: [b]–– Explanations:[/b][~LF][~LF]Lunar night works like our calendar date, but with nights: {s_mo-}x·y (p/z) where x = lunar {quotes|0-}month{quotes|1-} of the year, y = night of the lunar month. {s_mo-}1 is the first full moon of the light year (≈ Chinese New Year). As lunar months have different lengths, z indicates the total nights of the lunar month, p the full-moon night. The night ends at sunrise.[~LF][~LF]The light-year season divides the year into eight phases of increasing and decreasing light: 1. {solcal_lph|0-} ({solcal_dat0}), 2a. {solcal_lph|1-} ({solcal_dat1}), 2b. {solcal_lph|2-} ({solcal_dat2}), 2c. {solcal_lph|3-} ({solcal_dat3}), 3. {solcal_lph|4-} ({solcal_dat4}), 4a. {solcal_lph|5-} ({solcal_dat5}), 4b. {solcal_lph|6-} ({solcal_dat6}) and 4c. {solcal_lph|7-} ({solcal_dat7}). Short form: {s_li-}x·y (x = phase, y =[SP]% progress). Data are calculated astronomically.[DF][DF][DF]
txanleitung2: [DF]{txgordonssunclock} is essentially a single-hand clock with a dial that changes daily, oriented to the rhythms of nature and the celestial bodies. It shows the position of the Sun and the starry sky. A new kind of time display that is not artificial like our conventional clock (with railway time and daylight saving time), but moves in harmony with the seasons.[~LF][~LF][i]NOTE: You can swipe back and forth between this guide and the dial.[/i][~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– Your view:[/b][~LF][~LF]In the centre of the screen you see a horizontal line. This is the horizon (the Earth's surface). The pointed triangle in the middle is a spruce tree that you see from a distance (or a church). You are not looking down at it from above, but standing in front of it.[~LF][~LF]If you have not changed anything in the settings ({quotes|0-}{txuhrsinn-}: {txlangarr|auto-}{quotes|1-}), you are usually facing south in the Northern Hemisphere (north in the Southern Hemisphere). Objects on the left of the screen are in the east, on the right in the west (reversed in the Southern Hemisphere). The respective cardinal directions are shown at the edge of the horizon.[~LF][~LF]The lighter band below the horizon indicates civil twilight. The large dark grey area symbolises the night (including nautical twilight). Objects located in this area are not visible.[~LF][~LF]At the top right you see the local time (optional), the altitude of the Sun in degrees in the sky (e.g. 27.6°), as well as your position as a numerical code (e.g. {quotes|0-}cri-hs{quotes|1-}) or a place name if you have entered one. Below that the light season and the unequal temporal hour display.
txanleitung2b: [DF][DF][b]–– Sun:[/b][~LF][~LF]The [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]☉[/font] Sun (at the end of the clock’s single hand) moves during the day from left to right (reversed in the Southern Hemisphere). The further left the Sun appears on the screen, the more it is in the east; the further right, the more it is in the west (reversed in the Southern Hemisphere). The higher the Sun appears on the screen, the higher it stands in the sky.[~LF][~LF]The Sun follows the circular path drawn on the dial, along which it moves during the course of a day. At the edge of this circular path the hours are indicated (e.g. {quotes|0-}12{quotes|1-} o’clock), so that the circle also functions as a clock face.[~LF][~LF]On the clock face, for example, one can see how during daylight saving time the numbers shift while the circular path itself remains unchanged. Or: how legal time at most locations differs from local solar time, because 12 o’clock is not exactly at the top of the dial. Temporal hours work differently: at the 12th hour (12{s_td-}) it is always sunset.[~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– Moon:[/b][~LF][~LF]The [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]●[/font] Moon also has a circular path, shown in light grey. As the Moon needs more than 24 hours for one circuit, 1.5 hours from the previous day are added in very light grey. If one {quotes|0-}lives with the clock{quotes|1-}, one can nicely observe how the Moon rises higher and lower over a period of 27 days. One can also observe that at full moon it stands opposite the Sun on the circle (far away), while at new moon it occupies the same position on the circle (close to the Sun). Usually it is not exactly the same position because the view is from the side (not from above). The positions coincide exactly only during a solar eclipse.[~LF][~LF]Full moon and new moon are marked by shadows. New moon is shown for 48 hours because during this time the lunar crescent is usually not visible to the naked eye. Full moon is shown for 24 hours. Recalculation of the Sun and Moon paths takes place each day at UTC 00:00.
txanleitung2c: [DF][DF][b]–– Planets:[/b][~LF][~LF]The five planets visible to the naked eye are shown, depending on the design either with photos (as one would see them through binoculars) or with their astronomical symbols. When looking at the real night sky, you can distinguish planets from stars by knowing that planets do not twinkle but shine steadily. They appear noticeably larger in the sky than the distant stars and are therefore less affected by fluctuations in the density of the atmosphere.[~LF][~LF][font=DejaVuSans.ttf]♀[/font] Venus (pearl-coloured), [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]♂[/font] Mars (reddish), [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]♃[/font] Jupiter (striped), [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]♄[/font] Saturn (with rings), [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]☿[/font] Mercury (grey)[~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– Stars:[/b][~LF][~LF]In addition, the ten brightest stars are shown, represented by a letter inside a square. While the planets move in a relatively ordered way, mostly more or less close to the Sun’s path, the paths of the stars are independent of the Sun – after all, they are their own suns. In the star symbols, two sides of the square are drawn more strongly than the other two. This indicates the corner or quadrant of their path in which they are currently located. If the highlighted corner is at the bottom right, the star is (more or less) at the “end” of its path toward the bottom and right. In its further movement it will mainly move upward and to the left (often also outside the screen). If the upper side is stronger, the star is in front of you; if the lower side is stronger, it is behind you (provided you have not changed the rotation direction).
txmore2: [DF]&bl;s] Sirius, &bl;k] Canopus, &bl;f] Alpha Centauri, &bl;a] Arcturus, &bl;w] Vega, &bl;c] Capella, &bl;r] Rigel, &bl;p] Procyon, &bl;b] Betelgeuse, &bl;h] Achernar[~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– Weather:[/b][~LF][~LF]A local weather forecast can be displayed at the bottom left (details on the previous page below). It indicates the general character of the day through symbols: for example {wx/rain} stands for actual rain (6[NDASH]18 h), not occasional drizzle (similarly: {wx/rain2} heavy rain, {wx/snow} snow, {wx/uncertain} uncertain, {wx/dry} dry, {wx/sun} sunny, {wx/sun2} lots of sun, {wx/still} calm wind, {wx/wind} windy, {wx/storm} storm, {wx/storm2} gale). No symbol means an unremarkable weather situation. From bottom to top: current temperature, daily data (⁺ = new), precipitation (χ = snow) and hours of sunshine; weekday = tomorrow; grey = old data. The weather data are also used for atmospheric refraction and slightly affect the times of sunrise and sunset.[~LF][~LF][~LF][b]–– {txtimecontrol-}:[/b][~LF][~LF]If you want to move time forward or backward, press the [font=DejaVuSans.ttf]✸[/font] button. Then press and hold in the centre of the screen and slowly swipe to the right or left. The vertical position determines the step size: years at the top, minutes at the bottom. When released, the dial is recalculated (duration 1[NDASH]30 s). Double-tap to return to the present. If you want to go further back in time, for example to your birth sky, additional ephemeris data can be downloaded (years 1900[NDASH]2050, see next page).
txanleitung2d: [DF][DF][b]–– History:[/b][~LF][~LF]{txgordonssunclock} is the result of more than twenty years of engagement with the representation of time. The starting point was a mechanical organ clock with a 24 h dial and a month-dependent display of sunrise and sunset. From this emerged the concept of a {txsunclock-}, initially as a feasibility study and an ePaper wall clock; later, weather display and GPS were added. The idea of a location-flexible version culminated in this Android app.[~LF][~LF]The goal of the development is a representation of time that follows the course of the Sun and the natural daily and annual cycles – as a reconnection of humans with nature and with our inner, circadian time. May it provide insight and understanding.[DF][DF][DF]
txanleitung3: [DF]This software is a free, private project. Please use it as it is. In principle, no personal data is collected or transmitted. No internet connection is required for operation. Details on optional features (weather display, place name resolution), error reports, and update checks can be found in the privacy policy.[DF][DF]
txanleitung4: [DF]If you have found an error, I will gladly correct it. For this I need a description of where and when the error occurred (possibly with a screenshot). A screenshot of the log below taken as soon as possible after the error occurred is also helpful (mail contact: [CONTACTSUPPORT]). For {txtimecontrol} you can download additional ephemeris data here (and delete them again: ~17 MB, see previous page).[DF][DF]
txanleitung5: [DF]Thanks to: Brandon Rhodes (Skyfield), Rolf, Greg Miller, and all testers. Images: NASA/ SOHO, ESA/ Hubble, NASA/ JPL-Caltech (public domain). Weather data: Open-Meteo.com (CC BY 4.0), MET Norway (CC BY 4.0). Details: {quotes|0-}design/ COPYRIGHT.txt{quotes|1-}.[~LF][~LF]{txsunclock} is provided free of charge. Simply enjoy using it. If you would like to support my work, you can find my website here:[DF][DF]
